title: 2^n exceeds n^2 from n = 5 onward
informal_statement: For every natural number n >= 5, 2^n > n^2. False for n in {2,3,4} (e.g. 2^4 = 16 = 4^2), so the induction threshold genuinely starts at 5, not 0.
informal_proof_idea: Induct starting at n=5 via `Nat.le_induction` (not plain `induction n with zero | succ`, whose inductive hypothesis at the n=4-to-5 boundary would require the false premise `4 >= 5` to be usable — see the paired negative example negative.induction.two_pow_gt_sq_offbyone_naive_ih_failure.v1). Base case n=5: 32>25 by norm_num. Successor step: 2^(n+1) = 2*2^n > 2*n^2 >= (n+1)^2 for n>=5, closed by nlinarith with the inductive hypothesis and n>=5.
formal_statement_pp: theorem two_pow_gt_sq_from_five (n : ℕ) (h : n ≥ 5) : 2 ^ n > n ^ 2

review.review_basis: repo self-review; kernel-verified through the tracked proof-search loop

notes: Proof authority: Lean kernel via the tracked proof-search loop (episode 1fea172d). Step 1 (plain `induction n with zero | succ`, invoking `ih` via `ih (by omega)` assuming n>=5 follows from n+1>=5) kernel-failed and is preserved as negative.induction.two_pow_gt_sq_offbyone_naive_ih_failure.v1 — a genuine off-by-one pitfall: at the n=4 boundary, n+1>=5 holds but n>=5 does not, so the naive successor case cannot invoke ih. Steps 2-3 were tactic-transport/lemma-name fixes (raw_lean_block indentation, then Nat.le_induction syntax); step 4 closed kernel_verified with Nat.le_induction starting the induction at the correct threshold n=5.
